entry.title: Flink FileSystemTableSink: Code Walkthrough
entry.content:
Source Code Analysis - How Custom Partition Commit Policies Are Created and Invoked in Flink’s FileSystemTableSink

When writing to a filesystem via Flink’s Table API or SQL with custom partitioning enabled, how does the system inject and apply the sink.partition-commit.policy.kind parameter to control partition commit behavior? This article walks through the call path and implementation details from a source-code perspective.

My own implementation, [FLINK-32388] Add the ability to pass parameters to CUSTOM PartitionCommitPolicy (https://github.com/apache/flink/pull/22831), is also based on the source code analysis in this article.

Example:

1. Call Chain Overview: Where Does the Parameter Take Effect?

When a user submits a Flink SQL job, FileSystemTableSink participates in the construction of the physical execution plan as a TableSink. Its core parameter-handling logic occurs along the following call chain:

2. Physical Plan Translation: From Plan to Sink

When the Flink planner constructs the physical execution plan (Transformation or DataStream), it invokes the translateToPlanInternal method of the TableSink. For streaming writes to a filesystem, execution eventually reaches the consume method:

Whether the job runs in streaming mode is determined by whether the data is bounded (unbounded):

  • If streaming, it enters createStreamingSink to build the FileSystemSink;
  • Otherwise, createBatchSink is used for batch output.

3. Core of the Policy Chain Construction: createStreamingSink -> sink

The most important call inside createStreamingSink is the sink() method, which is responsible for writing data to the filesystem and executing the partition commit policy to ensure data consistency and visibility.

It ultimately creates the partition commit component PartitionCommitter and connects it to the writerStream:

4. Runtime Policy Chain Construction: PartitionCommitter

When the Flink job starts or is restored, PartitionCommitter.initializeState() is invoked automatically:

5. Core Logic of the PartitionCommitPolicyFactory

Creating the policy chain requires the user classloader (UserCodeClassloader) for the user-defined class and a filesystem factory (FileSystem factory), ensuring that policies can correctly load user code and operate on the partition’s filesystem.

💡 Key point: the custom class must implement the PartitionCommitPolicy interface and provide an appropriate constructor (either parameterized or no-arg).

Summary

The end-to-end injection flow for a parameter such as sink.partition-commit.policy.kind = metastore,success-file is as follows:

entry.title: Ingress and Route53 Issues with Druid AWS Deployments
entry.content:
Ingress and Route53 Issues with Druid AWS Deployments

Background

When deploying Druid with Kubernetes on AWS, it is common to use AWS ALB (Application Load Balancer) for traffic routing and load balancing. By configuring Kubernetes’ Ingress resource, ALB can route traffic to different services. At the same time, Route 53 needs to be configured to handle DNS domain name resolution so that applications can be accessed via domain names.

This configuration example shows how to set up the AWS ALB Ingress Controller to associate with the Kubernetes Ingress resource.

Debug

  1. Obtaining the ALB Certificate ARN: If you need to configure an SSL/TLS certificate for ALB, you can obtain the certificate ARN through AWS ACM (AWS Certificate Manager), which you need to request from the SRE team. This certificate is used for HTTPS configuration. The following is a sample configuration:

  2. Get Kubernetes Ingress address: Query the Ingress resource in Kubernetes via kubectl to get the ALB address. The following is a sample command and output:

    The ADDRESS field is the DNS address of the AWS ALB. Use it as a record for configuring Route 53.

  3. Modify the AWS Route 53 resource: Configure the appropriate A record or CNAME record for your domain name in AWS Route 53, ensuring that the druid-1.net and druid-2.net domain names point to the DNS address of the ALB (for example: internal-k8s-druid-xxx.elb.amazonaws.com).

    In the AWS Route 53 console, perform the following steps:

    • Create or update an A record that points to the DNS address of the ALB.
    • Ensure that the domain name resolution is set to Public or Private as required.

    Example record:

  4. Verify Configuration: After completing the ALB and Route 53 configuration, you can verify that the ALB is routing traffic properly by accessing domain names such as druid-1.net and druid-2.net.

Conclusion

With the ALB Ingress Controller and Route 53 properly configured, you can route traffic to services in Kubernetes and use AWS-provided certificates for HTTPS encryption. The key steps include obtaining the certificate ARN, querying the Ingress address, and updating the Route 53 configuration. Ensure that all resources and domains are configured correctly to access your application.

entry.title: Frame Size Configuration and RowTooLarge Error Resolution in Druid
entry.content:
Frame Size Configuration and RowTooLarge Error Resolution in Druid

Background

In Apache Druid, data processing usually involves dividing large-scale data into multiple “frames” for processing. In this process, the frame size is a key parameter that determines how much data can be processed in each frame. A frame size that is too large or too small can affect performance or cause processing to fail. This error log indicates that while processing a particular piece of data, a row of data was encountered whose size exceeded the specified frame size, causing the task to fail.

Error Message:

Indicates that while processing data, Druid encountered a row of data whose size exceeded 1,000,000 bytes (i.e., STANDARD_FRAME_SIZE ) and was unable to fit the current frame size limit.

Debug

  1. Frame Size Configuration: As you can see from the code snippet, Druid’s default frame size is 1,000,000 bytes. The value of STANDARD_FRAME_SIZE is statically defined:

    This value is used as the standard frame size for memory allocation during Druid task execution:

  2. Task Failure Reason: The task failed because a row of data exceeded the 1,000,000 byte size limit and could not fit into a single frame. This error message indicates that during execution, a row of data was too large for the set maximum frame size, causing the task to fail.

  3. Modifying the frame size: If larger rows of data need to be processed, consider increasing the size of STANDARD_FRAME_SIZE to allow larger rows of data to be processed per frame. For example, this could be modified to 2,000,000 or higher:

  4. Memory Optimization: Increasing the frame size may increase memory consumption, so there is a trade-off between memory usage and data processing performance. If there is not enough memory, increasing the frame size may cause problems such as memory overflow. When adjusting this value, you need to consider the overall system memory and load.

Conclusion

The task failed because a row of data exceeded the maximum frame size ( 1,000,000 bytes) configured by Druid. To resolve this issue, you can consider the following options:

  1. Increase the frame size: Increase STANDARD_FRAME_SIZE to a larger value, such as 2,000,000 or higher, to be able to handle larger rows of data.
  2. Optimize data row size: If possible, adjust how data is generated to reduce the size of a single row of data.
  3. Memory management: Increasing the frame size may increase memory consumption and you need to make sure that the system has enough memory available.

When adjusting the frame size, it is recommended to test it in conjunction with actual memory usage to avoid other problems caused by insufficient memory.

entry.title: Spark HistoryServer Intros and Troubleshooting
entry.content:
HistoryServer Introduction

HistoryServer is an HTTP service built into Spark, we can start it with the following code. When HistoryServer is started, it starts two threads to parse and clean the log files.

This creates a web interface at http://<server-url>:8080, listing incomplete and completed applications and attempts.

The spark jobs themselves must be configured to log events, and to log them to the same shared, writable directory.

For example, if the server was configured with a log directory of hdfs://namenode/spark-logs, then the client-side options would be:

To introduce the Spark History Server, the flowchart below outlines its workflow.

The Spark History Server relies on the Spark Event system. During a Spark task, various SparkListenerEvents are generated, like ApplicationStart and StageCompleted. These events are sent to the ListenerBus, where they are monitored by registered listeners. The EventLoggingListener writes these events to a JSON log file on a file system like HDFS.

The FsHistoryProvider in the History Server scans the log storage path periodically, extracts profile information (such as Application_id, user, status, start_time, end_time, event_log_path) from the log files, and maintains a list of this data. When a user accesses the UI, the system searches this list for the requested task and reads the corresponding event log file for details if it exists.

Issue

In some cases, after submitting a job using Spark, the log information corresponding to the appId cannot be found in the Spark HistoryServer WebUI (v3.2.1).

As a result, users cannot analyze the job log through the HistoryServer (HS). Upon analyzing the source code, it was found that the issue is due to the misuse of a data structure in a multithreading context.

Data Structure

Before explaining the problem, it’s important to understand the key data structure in HS. The misuse of this data structure causes the issue. HS uses a data structure called Listing to store key-value pairs, such as:

Log Parsing Process

The HS runs the checkForLogs method every 10 seconds by default to build the application list based on the current contents of the log directory. During each execution, this method scans the specified logDir and parses the log files to update its KVStore listing. The process includes two main methods:

  1. update/add log file records respectively
  2. remove stale log file records.

These methods are executed in different threads via submitLogProcessTask.

Issue Scenario

When checkForLogs is executed for the first time, the ABC job submitted by the user is still running. The file name in the log directory is ABC.inprocess, so the KVStore Listing adds two new entries:

In the next run of checkForLogs, now the ABC application has finished, the log file /tmp/ABC.inprocess has been deleted, and the new log file path is /tmp/ABC, so in this run of checkForLogs, two threads will start and do following things:

Thread 1: update/add log file records, this thread will parsing the log path /tmp/ABC. First it will update the <'ABC', InfoData<'ABC'>> in the KVstore Listing, second it will add a new record <'/tmp/ABC', InfoData<'/tmp/ABC'>> in KVstore Listing.

Thread 2: remove stale log file records, this thread will notice that the log path /tmp/ABC.inprocess has disappeared. First it will filter this path as a stale data, second it will start cleanAppData method, in this method the following code will be executed:

Usually, by the time this method loads the appId, Thread 1 has already updated <'ABC', InfoData<'ABC'>> in the KVStore Listing. Therefore, when the following code executes, the value of isStale will be false.

However, in some cases, when this method loads the appId, Thread 1 has not finished the update. As a result, when the code above executes, the value of isStale will be true. The worst outcome is that it will delete the log file record <'ABC', InfoData<'ABC'>> in the KVStore Listing after Thread 1 has completed the update.

So, the log file record <'ABC', InfoData<'ABC'>> will be permanently removed. When users access the WebUI for this application, they will see “Application not found” even though the log file for the app still exists.

One thing to note is that the log file record <'ABC', InfoData<'ABC'>> will not be added to the KVStore Listing again. In the next run of checkForLogs, the entry <'/tmp/ABC', InfoData<'/tmp/ABC'>> indicates that the log file of ABC does not need to be reloaded, so the method will skip this log file.

To better show the process of bug occurrence, below is the flowchart

Solution

Due to multi-thread error common use of KVstore Listing, we make the cleanAppData method atomic, just like the addListing mothod, this allows threads to use KVstore Listing without data errors.

For example:

If Thread 1 gets the lock on the KVstore Listing before Thread 2, it will update the record of <appId, InfoData<appId>>, so Thread 2 can safely get the record and it will make isStale be false, so the cleanAppData method will not delete the record in KVstore Listing.

If Thread 2 gets the lock on the KVstore Listing before Thread 1, it will make isStale be true, and the cleanAppData method will delete the record in KVstore Listing. But it will be ok, beacuse Thread 1 will add the record of <appId, InfoData<appId>> in KVstore Listing again.

In all situations above, the record of KVstore Listing will not remove unexpectedly.

This is the method used to fix the problem in Spark v3.2.2 (https://github.com/apache/spark/pull/36424/files).

entry.title: An Introduction to Kubernetes nodeAffinity & nodeSelector
entry.content:
Affinity-based Scheduling in Kubernetes

K8s POD is automatically scheduled to a NODE. By default, the scheduler considers whether resources are sufficient and tries to keep the load as evenly distributed as possible. However, sometimes we need finer-grained control over POD scheduling, for example, controlling whether PODs run on the same NODE. This is where a Kubernetes concept comes in: affinity. Affinity is mainly divided into two categories: nodeAffinity and podAffinity.

nodeSelector

Users can flexibly leverage label to manage resources in a cluster. The most common example is that a service selects PODs by matching label. Likewise, POD scheduling can also be targeted to specific deployments based on node labels.

We can view the labels of our nodes with the following command:

Now let’s first add a source=qikqiak label to node 192.168.1.140, with the following command:

We can use the --show-labels parameter above to check whether the label took effect. Once a node has been labeled, these labels can be used during scheduling. You simply add a nodeSelector field in the POD’s spec, containing the labels of the node you want the POD scheduled onto. For example, here is a default busybox POD YAML file from earlier:

Now if I want the above POD to be scheduled onto node 140, the simplest approach is to match the label on 140, as follows:

Then we can check the scheduling result via the describe command:

Looking at the information under Events, we can see that the POD above was correctly scheduled to node 140. From the example above, we can feel that the nodeSelector approach is fairly intuitive, but not flexible enough and too coarse-grained. Next we’ll look at another, more flexible approach: nodeAffinity.

nodeAffinity

nodeAffinity refers to node affinity, with its counterpart being Anti-Affinity. This approach is more flexible than the nodeSelector above, allowing for some simple logical combinations rather than just simple equality matching. Scheduling can be divided into soft and hard policies. A soft policy means that if there is no node that satisfies your scheduling requirements, the POD will ignore that rule and continue the scheduling process. In other words, it’s the “it’s best if it’s satisfied, but it doesn’t really matter if not“ policy. A hard policy is much stricter: if no node satisfies the conditions, it will keep retrying until the conditions are met. In short, it’s the “you must meet my requirements, otherwise I won’t do it“ policy.

nodeAffinity is divided into the following two policies:

  1. preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution — soft policy
  2. requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution — hard policy.

Here is an example: (test-node-affinity.yaml)

The above POD first requires that the POD cannot run on nodes 140 and 161, and if there is a node that satisfies source=qikqiak, it will be preferentially scheduled onto that node. Likewise, we can use the descirbe command to check whether the specific scheduling result meets our expectations. The matching logic here is that a label’s value is within a given list. The operators currently provided by Kubernetes are as follows:

  • In: the label’s value is in a given list
  • NotIn: the label’s value is not in a given list
  • Gt: the label’s value is greater than some value
  • Lt: the label’s value is less than some value
  • Exists: a label exists
  • DoesNotExist: a label does not exist

If there are multiple options under nodeSelectorTerms, satisfying any one of the conditions is enough; if there are multiple options in matchExpressions, all of these conditions must be satisfied simultaneously for the POD to be scheduled normally.

podAffinity

Both of the approaches above let a POD select a node. Sometimes we also want to perform scheduling based on relationships between PODs. The podAffinity concept introduced in Kubernetes 1.4 can fulfill this need.

Similar to nodeAffinity, podAffinity also has two scheduling policies: requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution and preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution. The only difference is that if we want to use mutual exclusion, we need to use the podAntiAffinity field. In the following example, we want with-pod-affinity and busybox-pod to be co-located, but we don’t want it to be deployed in the same topology domain as node-affinity-pod: (test-pod-affinity.yaml)

The POD in the example above needs to be scheduled onto a specific host on which at least one node is running a POD with an app=busybox-pod label. podAntiAffinity, on the other hand, means we’d prefer not to schedule onto a node that is running a POD with an app=node-affinity-pod label. Based on the definitions of the previous two PODs, we can predict that the POD above should be scheduled onto node 140, because busybox-pod was scheduled to node 140 while node-affinity-pod was scheduled to a node other than 140, which exactly satisfies the requirements above. Check with describe:

The event information above also confirms our reasoning.

At the same level as labelSelector and topologyKey, you can also define a namespaces list, indicating which namespaces’ pods to match. By default, it matches the namespace of the defined pod; if this field is defined but its value is empty, it matches all namespaces.

Check the results of the 3 PODs we defined above:

Comparison of affinity/anti-affinity scheduling policies:

Scheduling policyMatching labelOperatorsTopology domain supportScheduling target
nodeAffinityHostIn, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist, Gt, LtNoSpecified host
podAffinityPODIn, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExistYesPOD in the same topology domain as the specified POD
podAnitAffinityPODIn, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExistYesPOD not in the same topology domain as the specified POD

Taints and Tolerations

For nodeAffinity, whether hard or soft policy, the goal is to schedule the POD onto the expected node. Taints does the exact opposite: if a node is marked with a Taint, unless the POD is also marked as able to tolerate tainted nodes, no PODs will be scheduled to that tainted node.

For example, if users want to reserve the Master node for Kubernetes system components, or reserve a group of nodes with special resources for certain PODs, taints become very useful—PODs will no longer be scheduled onto nodes marked with a taint. Here is an example of tainting a node:

If you still want a POD to be scheduled onto a tainted node, you must define a Toleration in its Spec before it can be scheduled onto that node, as shown below:

There are three available options for effect, which can be set according to actual needs:

  1. NoSchedule: Unless there is a matching toleration specification, new Pods will not be scheduled onto the tainted node. Pods currently running on the node will not be evicted.
  2. PreferNoSchedule: PreferNoSchedule is the “preferred” or “soft” version of NoSchedule. The control plane will try to avoid scheduling Pods that cannot tolerate the taint onto the node, but this is not guaranteed to be completely avoided.
  3. NoExecute: This option means that once the Taint takes effect, any POD running on the node that does not have a matching Tolerate setting will be evicted directly.

Refer

  • https://kubernetes.io/zh-cn/docs/concepts/scheduling-eviction/taint-and-toleration/
  • https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/assign-pod-node/
  • https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/taint-and-toleration/
  • https://coreos.com/fleet/docs/latest/affinity.html

entry.title: Introduction to Kubernetes Probes
entry.content:
Probes Introduction

  • Liveness probes: Liveness probes are used to determine whether a container is healthy. If the health condition is not met, kubelet will decide whether to restart the Pod based on the restartPolicy configured on the Pod.

    A LivenessProbe performs checks according to its configuration (inspecting a process, a port, or whether a command executes successfully, etc.) to determine whether the container is functioning normally. If the probe fails (you can configure how many consecutive failures are required to mark the container as unhealthy), kubelet will kill the container and handle it according to the container’s restart policy. If no liveness probe is configured, the container is considered to have passed (Success) by default after startup — i.e., the probe always returns Success. Once Success is returned, the Pod status is RUNNING.

  • Readiness probes: Readiness probes are used to determine whether the program inside the container is alive (or healthy). Only when the program (service) is functioning normally will the container start accepting external network traffic (startup complete and ready).

    After the container starts, probing is performed according to the ReadinessProbe configuration. When no issues are detected, the result is Success. The Pod’s READY status becomes true, changing from 0/1 to 1/1. If probing fails, it remains 0/1 with status false. If no readiness probe is configured, the container is considered Success by default after startup. The relationships among this Pod, the Service resource associated with this Pod, and the EndPoint will also be set based on the Pod’s Ready status. If the Pod’s Ready state changes to false during operation, the system will automatically remove the Pod from the EndPoint list associated with the Service. At that point, when the Service resource receives a GET request, kube-proxy will definitely not route traffic into this Pod — this mechanism prevents traffic from being forwarded to unavailable Pods. If the Pod returns to the Ready state, it will be added back to the Endpoint list, and kube-proxy will probabilistically route traffic to this Pod through its load-balancing mechanism.

  • Startup Probes: For legacy applications that need a longer startup time, when you neither want to restart the application nor want requests to be routed in, you can configure a startup probe to grant sufficient startup time to ensure the application starts successfully.

Execution Order

You can customize whether these checks are executed when a Pod starts. If not configured, all checks default to passing.

If configured, the order is: startupProbe --> readinessProbe --> livenessProbe

But is this really the order? The official documentation says otherwise, stating:

Caution: Liveness probes do not wait for readiness probes to succeed. If you want to wait before executing a liveness probe you should use initialDelaySeconds or a startupProbe.

So the actual order is: startupProbe --> readinessProbe(livenessProbe)

readinessProbe and livenessProbe run asynchronously and concurrently.

LivenessProbe & ReadinessProbe Usage

Probe Methods

Both LivenessProbe and ReadinessProbe support the following three probing methods:

  • ExecAction: Execute a specified command inside the container. If the command succeeds with exit code 0, the probe succeeds.
  • HTTPGetAction: Invoke the HTTP GET method via the container’s IP address, port number, and path. If the response status code is greater than or equal to 200 and less than 400, the container is considered healthy.
  • TCPSocketAction: Perform a TCP check via the container’s IP address and port number. If a TCP connection can be established, the container is considered healthy.

Probe Parameters

Related attributes of the LivenessProbe and ReadinessProbe probes.
A Probe has many optional fields that can be used to more precisely control the behavior of Liveness and Readiness probes:

  • initialDelaySeconds: Number of seconds to wait after the container starts before the probe begins working. Unit: “seconds”. Default is 0, minimum is 0.

  • periodSeconds: The interval at which probing is performed (in seconds). Default is 10s, unit “seconds”, minimum is 1.

  • timeoutSeconds: The timeout for waiting for a response after the probe issues a check request. Default is 1s, unit “seconds”, minimum is 1.

  • successThreshold: The minimum number of consecutive successes required for the probe to be considered successful after having failed. Default is 1, in Liveness probes it must be 1, minimum is 1.

  • failureThreshold: The number of retries before a probe is considered failed. After a certain number of retries, it is considered failed. In readiness probes, the Pod will be marked as not ready. Default is 3, minimum is 1.

    Tips: initialDelaySeconds does not actually need to be configured in ReadinessProbe. Without configuration, by default ReadinessProbe begins probing immediately after the Pod starts. Once the service has started and the check passes, the READY status naturally becomes normal.

Example

Refer

  • https://juejin.cn/post/7163135179177852936
  • https://juejin.cn/post/7163135453489528845

entry.title: Debugging Oversized Systemd Journal Logs
entry.content:
Background

Our machines have been frequently triggering disk capacity alerts recently, and we found that the following directories were consuming a significant amount of disk space:

/run/log/journal is the systemd log directory, used to store systemd journal files. You can use the journalctl tool to manage and clean up these log files. Below is how to clean up systemd logs.

Solution

1. List log entries:

To view the available log entries, use the following command:

This will display the currently available systemd logs.

2. Configure log retention policy:

Systemd uses a log retention policy to manage how long log files are kept. You can configure the retention policy by editing the /etc/systemd/journald.conf file. Look for the SystemKeepFree, SystemMaxUse, and RuntimeMaxUse options, then set them according to your needs. For example:

This configures the maximum disk space used by systemd journal logs to 100MB. You can adjust this value to suit your requirements.

3. Clean up logs:

Once you have set the retention policy, you can run the following command to clean up the logs:

This will delete logs older than the specified time, keeping only the most recent ones.You can change the value of the **`--vacuum-time`** flag to define how long logs should be retained.

4. Verify the cleaned-up log size:

To verify the size of the log files after cleanup, run the following command again:

This will display the currently available log information, including the portion that has been cleaned up.

Tips

Note that cleaning up systemd logs requires sufficient privileges. Make sure you have the necessary permissions to perform these operations, or use the sudo command to execute them with administrator privileges. Cleaning up log files may free up disk space, but it may also result in the loss of some older log information. Therefore, choose your cleanup strategy carefully based on your needs and available storage.

entry.title: Debugging Kubernetes FailedScheduling
entry.content:
Background

While deploying Druid on Kubernetes, I noticed that after changing the replica count of the StatefulSet, the pod remained stuck in Pending. Running kubectl describe on the pod surfaced the following logs.

Debug

My first instincts pointed to one of the following bugs:

  1. Incorrect labels on the nodegroup resource
  2. Incorrect labels on the StatefulSet resource
  3. Auto-scaling on the nodegroup failing to trigger

After inspecting the dedicated label and tolerations on both the StatefulSet and the nodegroup, nothing looked wrong, which ruled out Bug 1 and Bug 2. That left Bug 3 as the focus.

The nodegroup already contained a node. If capacity were the issue, there should be scale-up attempt logs in the nodegroup’s activities — but there weren’t any. So scaling out would not have solved this either, which ruled out Bug 3 as well.

Conclusion

The root cause turned out to be that the nodegroup’s instance type had too little memory: even if the autoscaler did scale up, no node in that group could offer enough memory for a single pod, which triggered the errors above.
These logs easily mislead you into thinking the problem is a label mismatch or a broken autoscaler — a very sneaky bug.

entry.title: HDFS Decommission Codes and Introduction
entry.content:
HDFS Decommission Codes and Introduction

Background of HDFS decommission

Currently there are 200+ nodes of 1.22PB HDFS3 and fewer than 100 nodes of 480T HDFS. The decommissioning of nodes is slow, migrating only about 150k blocks per hour on average. A node has about 600k blocks on average, and decommissioning one node takes roughly 4 hours.

Difference between node drop and node decommissioning

Node Decommissioning

First, add the node to the decommissioning list. This tells the NameNode and YARN to stop submitting new tasks and stop writing data to it. Then wait for the blocks on that node to be replicated throughout the cluster. During this time the decommissioning node is preferred as the srcNode (data source), because it has no write requests and its load is low. Other nodes copy blocks from this decommissioning node, so its load will be high during this period. Once all blocks have been replicated, the node state becomes Decommissioned, which can be viewed from the NameNode URL interface.

Note that a decommissioning DataNode also starts replicating 10 minutes and 30 seconds later. It does not start earlier just because decommissioning was actively initiated. The NameNode and DataNode always maintain a simple master/slave relationship: the NameNode never actively initiates any IPC call to the DataNode. Any operation the DataNode must cooperate on is returned via the DatanodeCommand carried on the heartbeat reply.

Node Drop

For example, forcibly stopping a DataNode, the physical machine dying (high load drop, sudden network failure, hardware failure, etc.) are all node drops. By default, 10 minutes 30 seconds later (mainly controlled by two parameters) the NameNode will detect that communication with the node is abnormal and mark it as dropped. The NameNode then looks up all blockids for that node (by IP) and the replica-holding machines, and schedules replication via the heartbeat mechanism. At this point the data source for replication is no longer the dropped node itself, but one of the other nodes holding a replica. Replica placement still follows rack awareness and the replica placement policy.

💡 The difference between a node drop and decommissioning is not only the replication data-source selection; the NameNode's under-replicated-block replication strategy is also different (block replication priority is divided into five levels). As an extreme example, decommissioning a single-replica node will not lose data, whereas a drop of a single-replica node really does lose data.

Network storm caused by a node drop

A node carrying tens of TB, or even hundreds of TB, and millions of blocks going offline will produce a large RPC storm. This is especially challenging for the NameNode in large, high-load clusters: it not only impacts production performance but also creates significant risk, particularly for clusters with bandwidth bottlenecks.

Generally, the NameNode detects a DataNode drop after 103s (heartbeat time) + 25min (NameNode check time, parameter dfs.namenode.heartbeat.recheck-interval) = 10min30s. If within those 10min30s the bandwidth stays saturated, RPC requests are delayed, and DataNode-to-NameNode communication degrades, it is easy for other nodes to keep falling offline, forming a vicious cycle. How should this be avoided?

The NameNode maintains a replication priority queue. For blocks with insufficient replicas, the block with only one replica remaining gets the highest replication priority. So in a cluster with replication factor 2, as soon as one replica goes wrong there is only one replica left, which is the highest priority, and it will be replicated in storm mode. If not controlled well, this can easily affect cluster performance or even bring the cluster down. It is therefore generally not recommended to run a cluster with a replication factor of 2.

💡 L1 (highest): blocks at risk of data loss, e.g. 1) blocks with only one replica (especially 2-replica blocks after one node goes down) or blocks with 0 active replicas; 2) single-replica blocks owned by a node currently being decommissioned.

L2: blocks whose actual replica count is far below the configured value (e.g., 3-replica blocks missing 2), i.e., blocks whose replica count drops below 1/3 of the expected value. These blocks are replicated with the second priority. For example, a 4-replica block missing 3 replicas is replicated before a 4-replica block missing only 2.

L3: under-replicated blocks whose shortage is less severe than L2. Third priority.

L4: blocks that already meet the minimum replica count requirement. Their replication need is lower than L2 and L3.

L5: corrupt blocks that currently have an available non-corrupt replica.

Parameters controlling RPC storms caused by node drops

All three parameters live in hdfs-site.xml. Refer to the Apache Hadoop official website for details. Block replication speed is determined by two aspects: the rate at which the NameNode dispatches tasks, and the rate at which DataNodes replicate between themselves. The former can be thought of as the entrance; the latter as the exit.

These parameters are not available in Hadoop 2.7.3.

Ingress Parameters

Control task dispatch from the NameNode side. Changing this parameter requires a restart of the NameNode, not the DataNode.

This parameter determines how many blocks the NameNode tells each DataNode to replicate on each heartbeat (3s) when it sends the task list. For example, in a 500-node cluster with this value set to 10, one heartbeat allows the NameNode to tell DataNodes to replicate 10*500=5000 blocks.

If a node drops/decommissions with 800,000 blocks needing replication, how long does it take the NameNode to finish dispatching all replication tasks to DataNodes?

Egress Parameters

In contrast to the above, which controls dispatch from the NameNode, the two parameters below control things at the DataNode level. They also require a restart of the NameNode.

This parameter controls the maximum number of replication threads on a DataNode. As mentioned above, block replication priority has five levels. This parameter controls replication of blocks other than the highest priority.

This parameter controls the number of replication streams for blocks of all priorities on a DataNode, including the highest priority. It is typically used together with the two parameters above.

💡 The former parameter controls how frequently a DataNode accepts tasks, while the latter two further cap the maximum parallel threaded network transfer a DataNode can perform at once. The appropriate values depend on cluster size and configuration and cannot be generalized. It is usually simpler to control things from the ingress side. For example, with a 500-node cluster and `dfs.namenode.replication.work.multiplier.per.iteration=10`, a single heartbeat dispatches 5000 blocks. If cluster files are spread across all 500 nodes and each replicates 10 blocks at once (in practice, rack awareness and replica placement policy mean not all nodes participate), and each block is 128MB, then per-node network load is 128 * 10 / 3 = 546 Mb/s. You then need to check whether that exceeds your actual bandwidth bottleneck and whether such large network IO would affect regular task computation. If so, tune this value down.

How to decommission quickly

The essence of making nodes decommission quickly is to improve replica replication speed. This is mainly controlled by the three parameters above: the first controls NameNode task dispatch, the other two control DataNode replication rate, with the precondition that normal production tasks are not affected. The smaller the cluster, the slower decommissioning, because the overall dispatch volume per heartbeat is lower.

For instance, on a 500-node cluster dfs.namenode.replication.work.multiplier.per.iteration=10; on a 50-node cluster this value should be set to 100 so that NameNode dispatch speed is comparable. Tune to your actual cluster size.

In a test cluster of 15 HDFS nodes with ~80k blocks each on average, migration reached 63k blocks per hour, a 5x speedup over the initial 12k.

Impact

Significantly increases CPU Load, CPU IO Wait, Disk IO Wait, and other resource utilization.

Source Code Analysis

Once the address in dfs.hosts.exclude is written to the configuration, refreshNodes can read the exclude list and act on it.

Node addresses are read via dfs.exclude, the nodes are marked as decommissioning, and a monitor is used to track them.

Before decommissioning, cluster stats are pre-deducted, for example capacityUsed, capacityTotal, and so on.

The monitor’s run() calls processPendingNodes() to take nodes from pendingNodes and decommission them.

After obtaining nodes, check() is called, which retrieves block information for these nodes and processes the pending replication blocks.

  1. processPendingReplication() calls isBlockReplicatedOk() (the core), which decides whether a block needs reconstruction and pushes information into the replication queue within blockManager.
  1. moveBlocksToPending() checks the queue length and the pending-replication limit, creates a block iterator for each node that needs maintenance or decommissioning, and then for every block keeps calling isBlockReplicatedOk() until the replication limit is reached, at which point it stops and exits the loop.

There is an interesting piece of locking logic inside moveBlocksToPending():

It releases the write lock and immediately reacquires it. The blocksProcessed variable tracks how many blocks have been processed. Once it reaches the blocksPerLock threshold, releasing and reacquiring the write lock gives other threads waiting on the write lock a chance to run. This avoids holding the write lock for long periods and blocking other threads, improving concurrency.

💡

Why does check call processPendingReplication first and then moveBlocksToPending? Can it just call one?

In some cases only one function is enough to handle a block’s replication state, but in other cases both are needed. Here is why both calls are necessary in some scenarios:

  1. processPendingReplication:
    • Its main job is to handle blocks currently in the “pending replication” state and ensure they reach the required replica count. It is usually invoked periodically by the scheduler to keep cluster blocks sufficiently redundant.
    • When called, it checks which blocks are currently pending and tries to start replication tasks for them.
  2. moveBlocksToPending:
    • Its main job is to move blocks into the “pending replication” queue under specific conditions, for example when a node enters maintenance mode or goes offline, so they can be picked up by subsequent replication tasks.
    • When called, it checks whether any node is currently in maintenance or offline, and then adds the relevant blocks to the pending-replication queue.

Why call both at the same time? Because they do not address the same problem:

  • processPendingReplication focuses on blocks that are already pending, while moveBlocksToPending focuses on the blocks that need to be handled after a node’s state changes.
  • At any moment new blocks may need replicating, or blocks may need to be moved into the pending queue because a node’s state changed. Calling both ensures replication-state handling is comprehensive and timely.
  1. checkForCompletedNodes iterates over node states and, if a node’s block migration is complete, adds the node to the toRemove list.
  2. processCompletedNodes takes the toRemove list, iterates through it, and sets final state (for example decommissioned), removing the node from outOfServiceNodeBlocks and pendingRep.
